text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH IV AND ORAL CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma and synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases (KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS). End-of-induction imaging following completion of 12 cycles of modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab. Oxaliplatin discontinued at completion of planned 12-cycle course due to cumulative peripheral neurotoxicity. Assessment of treatment response and hepatic resectability for multidisciplinary surgical planning.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired in portal venous phase following administration of 100 mL Omnipaque-350 IV contrast. Oral contrast administered. Multiplanar reformations performed. Images compared to prior CT abdomen and pelvis performed after Cycle 8 and to the baseline staging CT.

---

FINDINGS

CHEST:
Lungs: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules are identified. No pleural effusions. No consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or evidence of pulmonary parenchymal disease.
Mediastinum: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. Heart size is normal. No pericardial effusion.
Chest wall: Subcutaneous port-a-cath device is present in the right anterior chest wall with the tip in the expected position of the cavoatrial junction. No periprocedural complication identified.
Bones (thorax): No suspicious osseous lesions.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology. There is no ascites. Four hepatic lesions are again identified, all demonstrating continued interval decrease in size relative to all prior studies:

  1. Segment VI (right lobe): Hypoenhancing mass measuring 1.6 x 1.4 cm in axial dimensions (previously 2.0 x 1.7 cm after Cycle 8; 3.2 x 2.8 cm at baseline). Persistent low-attenuation on portal venous phase without arterial enhancement. There is no rim enhancement or central liquefaction to suggest frank necrosis. Margins appear relatively well-defined. This lesion has undergone substantial volumetric reduction across the treatment course. No satellite nodularity immediately surrounding the lesion.

  2. Segment IVb: Near-complete radiographic resolution. A small 0.7 x 0.5 cm focus of subtle hypoattenuation is identified at the expected location. Attenuation characteristics approach that of treated/fibrotic tissue and the lesion is near the threshold of CT detectability. Previously measured 0.9 x 0.8 cm after Cycle 8.

  3. Segment II (left lobe): Similar near-complete radiographic response. A 0.5 x 0.4 cm ill-defined area of subtle hypoattenuation is noted, markedly diminished from the 1.4 x 1.1 cm lesion identified at baseline. This likely represents residual fibrosis/treated tissue or a minimally viable deposit at or below reliable CT detection.

  4. Segment VII: No discrete lesion is identified at the location of the previously described 0.9 x 0.8 cm lesion (baseline). The area is now isoattenuating to adjacent hepatic parenchyma. Cannot exclude a small treated focus below the resolution of CT.

Overall assessment of hepatic disease: Substantial and sustained reduction in hepatic metastatic burden over the course of 12 cycles of induction chemotherapy. The dominant Segment VI lesion remains the most conspicuous residual finding. Three of four original lesions have undergone near-complete or complete radiographic response.

No new hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent. No hepatic vein thrombosis.

Note: The liver parenchyma demonstrates mild heterogeneity in attenuation, with some subtle hypoattenuation in the right lobe periphery which may reflect chemotherapy-related parenchymal changes (sinusoidal obstruction syndrome / hepatic sinusoidal injury related to oxaliplatin exposure), though this is a nonspecific finding. No features of frank hepatic steatosis.

Gallbladder: No gallstones. No cholecystitis. Normal wall thickness.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size (10.4 cm craniocaudal). No splenic lesions.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adrenal masses.

Kidneys and ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. A 3 mm left renal cortical cyst is unchanged from prior. Normal bilateral ureteral course.

Retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes. Previously noted pericolonic nodal disease has resolved.

Primary tumor and sigmoid colon: Circumferential wall thickening of the sigmoid colon is again identified, now measuring approximately 2.8 x 2.4 cm in cross-section (previously 3.1 x 2.7 cm after Cycle 8; 4.1 x 3.8 cm at baseline). There has been a modest but progressive decrease in the degree of mural thickening over successive imaging studies. The mucosal enhancement pattern appears somewhat improved with less aggressive pericolonic haziness compared to baseline. Pericolonic fat stranding is substantially reduced. The previously enlarged pericolonic lymph nodes are no longer visualized. No evidence of bowel obstruction. No fistula formation. No perforation.

The sigmoid mass remains present and bulky. While treatment has resulted in meaningful dimensional reduction, residual viable tumor cannot be excluded on imaging grounds alone given that partial response (rather than complete response) has been the pattern throughout treatment.

Pelvis: No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Bladder is unremarkable. Prostate gland is mildly enlarged but without discrete nodule (age-appropriate appearance). No suspicious pelvic osseous lesions.

Peritoneum: No peritoneal nodularity. No omental caking or ascites. No peritoneal carcinomatosis.

Vasculature: The aorta and its major branches are patent. No significant atherosclerotic disease. No thrombosis.

Bones: Visualized thoracic, lumbar, and pelvic osseous structures are unremarkable. No sclerotic or lytic lesions to suggest osseous metastatic involvement.

---

IMPRESSION

1. HEPATIC METASTASES -- SIGNIFICANT ONGOING TREATMENT RESPONSE: All four previously identified hepatic metastatic lesions have undergone substantial reduction from baseline values across the course of 12-cycle induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab. The dominant Segment VI lesion (right lobe) remains the most identifiable residual deposit, now 1.6 x 1.4 cm -- markedly reduced from 3.2 x 2.8 cm at initial staging. The Segment IVb and Segment II lesions are near-complete radiographic responders, each measuring below 1 cm with attenuation characteristics approaching treated tissue. The Segment VII lesion is no longer discretely visualized and may represent radiographic complete response at that site. Three of four lesions at or near radiographic complete response; one dominant lesion persists.

2. SIGMOID COLON PRIMARY TUMOR -- PARTIAL TREATMENT RESPONSE: The primary sigmoid mass is appreciably smaller than at baseline (2.8 x 2.4 cm vs. 4.1 x 3.8 cm), with improvement in pericolonic nodal disease (now resolved) and reduced pericolonic inflammation. Residual mural thickening is present, consistent with either viable residual tumor or treatment-related fibrosis; distinguishing these cannot be reliably accomplished by CT morphology alone.

3. NO NEW SITES OF METASTATIC DISEASE: No pulmonary nodules, no peritoneal implants, no osseous lesions, and no new hepatic deposits are identified. No extrahepatic spread at this time.

4. MILD HEPATIC PARENCHYMAL HETEROGENEITY: Subtle right lobe parenchymal heterogeneity is noted; correlation with liver function tests and clinical context is advised given oxaliplatin exposure history (sinusoidal injury). This does not represent a contraindication to hepatic surgery but should be factored into surgical risk planning.

5. SUBCUTANEOUS PORT-A-CATH: In appropriate position, no complication.

The overall imaging picture is consistent with the best response achieved following completion of planned induction chemotherapy. Multidisciplinary hepatobiliary surgical evaluation is recommended to determine feasibility of hepatic resection and/or ablative consolidation of residual disease, particularly the Segment VI lesion. The primary sigmoid tumor will also require eventual surgical consideration.

Compared to baseline staging CT and both interim studies (post-Cycle 4 and post-Cycle 8), this study represents the maximal response achieved during the induction phase.

Correlating tumor markers: CEA 22.8 ng/mL (markedly reduced from 148.2 at baseline); CA 19-9 89 U/mL (reduced from 312 U/mL at baseline).

Report reviewed and electronically signed.
Radiology Attending: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Section]
